text:
REFINED BRIEFING (events grouped by probative direction).
Retrieval hints: actors=['U.S. Department of State', 'U.S. Department of the Treasury', 'International Criminal Court'], topics=['sanctions', 'icc', 'foreign-policy', 'international-law', 'israel'].

## SUPPORTS-YES (5)
- [2025-01-20] Donald J. Trump was inaugurated as the 47th President of the United States on January 20, 2025. Hours after taking office, President Trump issued an 'America First Trade Policy' memorandum and several executive orders. The memorandum outlines priorities including expanded use of tariffs, reviews of export controls, and an assessment of outbound investment policies. Trump also rescinded President Biden's Executive Order 14110 on Artificial Intelligence, re-designated Cuba as a State Sponsor of Terrorism, reinstated penalties against International Criminal Court personnel, and rescinded sanctions related to the West Bank. He also issued an executive order designating certain international drug cartels, such as MS-13 and Tren de Aragua, as Foreign Terrorist Organizations and Specially Designated Global Terrorists.

## BACKGROUND (5)
- [2025-01-20] On January 20, 2025, President Donald Trump signed several executive actions shortly after taking office.

One order directed the U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations to formally notify the UN Secretary-General of the United States' immediate withdrawal from the Paris Agreement on climate change. This marked the second time Trump moved to exit the accord, which the U.S. had rejoined in 2021. The order also revoked the U.S. International Climate Finance Plan.

Another executive order, "Unleashing American Energy," aimed to boost fossil fuel production and reverse Biden-era climate policies. It withdrew 12 climate change-related executive orders, terminated the American Climate Corps, and directed agencies to review and rescind regulations burdening domestic energy development, including pausing funds from the Inflation Reduction Act.

Trump also issued a "Regulatory Freeze Pending Review" memorandum, directing all executive departments and agencies to halt new rulemaking processes. Agencies were ordered not to propose or issue new rules until approved by new appointees, to withdraw unpublished rules, and to consider postponing effective dates of finalized but not yet effective rules for 60 days.

Separately, Executive Order 14148, "Initial Rescissions of Harmful Executive Orders and Actions," revoked over 60 executive orders and memoranda from the previous administration, targeting policies on diversity, equity, and inclusion, border management, and climate. It also reinstated Cuba's designation as a state sponsor of terrorism.
